refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles agreed with customer). Measure end-to-end packet loss across Telemetry and Tasking interfaces during simulated and live teleoperation scenarios. Document network conditions and loss rates; confirm ≤1% threshold is met.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on operator control.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission on Telemetry and Tasking streams, tuned to customer network profile.
DP-C: Establish quality-of-service (QoS) prioritization and traffic shaping to protect critical teleoperation packets from congestion-induced loss.
DP-D: Combine DP-B and DP-C: implement FEC/retransmission with QoS prioritization to address both random loss and congestion-induced loss.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach combining adaptive forward error correction (FEC) and selective retransmission on Telemetry and Tasking streams, integrated with QoS prioritization and traffic shaping to protect critical teleoperation packets under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation packet loss mitigation to network infrastructure (QoS support required). Dependency on customer network capability to enforce QoS policies.
FEC and retransmission logic must be coordinated across Telemetry (sensor data inbound) and Tasking (command data outbound) interfaces; potential for asymmetric loss handling.
Adaptive tuning of FEC overhead and retransmission timeout requires characterization of customer network profile; tight coupling to network conditions assumption.
coupling_check.notes: Coupling is acceptable because: (1) QoS is a standard network service; (2) FEC/retransmission are localized to Teleoperation module; (3) Telemetry and Tasking interfaces are already defined system boundaries. Recommend explicit network profile characterization as a design input.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer network supports QoS tagging and prioritization (e.g., DSCP marking, traffic classes).
ASSUMPTION: Packet loss is primarily random or congestion-induced, not due to systematic link failures or extreme latency.
RISK: If customer network does not support QoS, DP-D effectiveness is reduced; fallback to DP-B (FEC/retransmission only) may be insufficient to meet ≤1% target.
RISK: Adaptive FEC overhead increases bandwidth consumption; may conflict with bandwidth-constrained networks.
RISK: Retransmission introduces latency; must be tuned to avoid operator control lag (typically <500ms round-trip for safe teleoperation).
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify: (1) FEC code rate and algorithm (e.g., Reed-Solomon, Turbo codes); (2) retransmission timeout and max retry count; (3) QoS marking scheme (DSCP values); (4) fallback behavior if QoS is unavailable. Conduct network characterization study with customer to validate assumptions and tune parameters.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the implementation of packet loss mitigation logic, including FEC encoding/decoding, selective retransmission, and QoS marking on outbound Telemetry and Tasking streams. The module interfaces with the Network Link to transmit/receive packets and with Safety Services to report packet loss events that may trigger safety alerts.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional sensor telemetry stream (inbound from Vehicle) and telemetry feedback (outbound to Tele-operator). FEC and retransmission applied to protect inbound telemetry quality.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional command tasking stream (outbound to Vehicle) and task acknowledgment (inbound from Vehicle). FEC and retransmission applied to protect outbound tasking quality.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport layer. Teleoperation module applies QoS marking and monitors packet loss statistics on this interface.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound alerts to Safety Services when packet loss exceeds thresholds or retransmission exhaustion occurs, triggering operator notification or equipment shutdown.

module_containment_scores.notes: Teleoperation is the natural owner of packet loss mitigation for teleoperation streams. FEC/retransmission logic is cohesive and localized. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert notification crosses module boundary (acceptable).

Safety Services owns safety policy and alerting, but packet loss mitigation (FEC, retransmission, QoS) is not a safety function per se; it is a communication quality function. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would scatter communication logic across modules.

Vehicle owns equipment control and onboard telemetry. Packet loss mitigation is a remote communication concern, not a vehicle-local function. Assigning to Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle firmware and network protocol logic.

NGHE Solution is the system-level container. While it could own packet loss mitigation at a high level, the actual implementation (FEC, retransmission, QoS) is best localized to the Teleoperation module to maintain separation of concerns and enable independent testing.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner because: (1) it already owns the Telemetry and Tasking interfaces; (2) FEC/retransmission logic is cohesive and communication-specific; (3) it minimizes diffusion (only SafetyAlert notification crosses boundary); (4) it enables independent testing and tuning of packet loss mitigation without affecting Vehicle or Safety Services logic.
containment_risks: RISK: If Teleoperation module is not yet fully defined or has unclear boundaries, assigning DP-D may overload it. Recommend confirming Teleoperation module scope and interface contracts before detailed design.
RISK: SafetyAlert interface coupling: if Safety Services is slow to respond to packet loss alerts, operator may not be notified in time. Recommend defining SafetyAlert latency SLA (e.g., <100ms).

RISK: Network Link interface abstraction: if Network Link does not expose QoS marking or packet loss statistics, Teleoperation module cannot fully implement DP-D. Recommend confirming Network Link API supports these capabilities.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Confirm that the Teleoperation module interface contract includes: (1) Telemetry and Tasking stream definitions (packet format, rate, size); (2) Network Link API for QoS marking and loss statistics; (3) SafetyAlert message format and latency SLA. Conduct a design review with Teleoperation module owner to validate feasibility of FEC/retransmission implementation within module scope.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid FEC/retransmission + QoS) directly addresses the refined FR requirement to maintain ≤1% packet loss. The DP is specific, measurable, and traceable to the acceptance criterion. Coupling analysis confirms DP-D is acceptable with documented assumptions (QoS support, network profile characterization).

diffusion_risks: SafetyAlert notification introduces a cross-module dependency. Mitigation: define SafetyAlert latency SLA and test alert propagation end-to-end.
Network Link interface must expose QoS marking and loss statistics. Mitigation: confirm Network Link API contract before detailed design.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module containment score of 95 is strong. FEC/retransmission logic is cohesive and communication-specific. Minimal diffusion (SafetyAlert only) is acceptable. Recommend confirming Teleoperation module scope and interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification is feasible via packet loss measurement under representative network conditions. Recommend establishing a test harness that simulates customer network profiles (latency, bandwidth, loss rates) and measures end-to-end packet loss on Telemetry and Tasking streams. Acceptance test: confirm ≤1% loss under agreed conditions.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Teleoperation module owns packet loss mitigation. Ownership boundary is well-defined. Recommend documenting allocation in system architecture document and design specification.

recommended_revisions: Refine DP-D specification to include FEC algorithm, code rate, retransmission timeout, QoS marking scheme, and fallback behavior.
Conduct network characterization study with customer to validate assumptions and tune DP-D parameters.
Confirm Teleoperation module interface contract (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert) before detailed design.
Define SafetyAlert latency SLA (e.g., <100ms) to ensure timely operator notification.
Establish packet loss measurement test harness and acceptance test criteria.

decision.justification: The refined FR is well-scoped and the selected DP is sound with acceptable coupling. The Teleoperation module is the optimal owner with high containment score (95). However, the FR is not atomic—it decomposes into three sub-FRs (packet loss detection, error correction, graceful degradation). Recommend decomposing to the next level to detail each sub-FR and ensure comprehensive coverage of packet loss mitigation strategy.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall detect and report packet loss events in real-time (detection mechanism, reporting latency, alert thresholds).

FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall implement error correction or retransmission mechanisms to minimize effective packet loss impact on operator control (FEC algorithm, retransmission policy, latency budget).
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall gracefully degrade or alert the operator when packet loss exceeds acceptable thresholds (degradation modes, alert types, operator actions).

assumptions: Customer network supports QoS tagging and prioritization (DSCP, traffic classes).
Packet loss is primarily random or congestion-induced, not systematic link failures.
Teleoperation module is fully defined with clear interface contracts (Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert).
Network Link API exposes QoS marking and packet loss statistics.
Operator control latency budget allows for retransmission timeout (typically <500ms round-trip).
Safety Services can respond to SafetyAlert notifications within acceptable latency (e.g., <100ms).
open_risks: Customer network may not support QoS; fallback to FEC/retransmission only may be insufficient.
Adaptive FEC overhead may exceed bandwidth constraints in some network conditions.
Retransmission latency may conflict with operator control responsiveness requirements.
SafetyAlert notification latency may delay operator awareness of packet loss events.
Network Link interface may not expose required QoS and loss statistics APIs.
evidence_needed: Network characterization study: customer network profile (latency, bandwidth, loss rates, QoS support).
Teleoperation module interface contract: Telemetry, Tasking, Network Link, SafetyAlert definitions.
FEC algorithm selection and performance analysis (code rate, latency, overhead).
Retransmission policy design and latency impact analysis.
SafetyAlert latency SLA and propagation testing.
Packet loss measurement test harness and acceptance test results.
